comprehensive_summary:
Nicolás Guillén (full name Nicolás Cristóbal Guillén Batista) was a Cuban poet and journalist of African descent, declared Cuba's national poet by the Castro government in 1961, a position he held for over two decades. He is overwhelmingly identified in quizbowl through his poem "Sensemayá" (also known as "Chant for Killing a Snake"), which features the onomatopoeic refrain "Mayombe-bombe-mayombe" and describes a snake-killing ritual. In that poem, a figure with "eyes of glass" "coils on a stick" and "can move without feet," and the speaker instructs "Hit him with an ax and he dies." The poem inspired a musical composition of the same name by Mexican composer Silvestre Revueltas, which begins with a bass clarinet trill in 4/8 time. Eartha Kitt recorded a reading of Langston Hughes's translation of the poem.

Guillén drew on the rhythms of the musical genre son in his collections Motivos de son and El son entero. His first gained fame with eight short poems in Motivos de son based on Afro-Cuban son music; the collection's first poem addresses a boy of Afro-Cuban ethnicity, telling him "Charity's payin' yo' keep / She's givin' you all you need." Hughes translated poems from Motivos de son into Black vernacular English, including "Li'l Manuel," who is critiqued for "all dat English you used to know." His collection Songoro cosongo is noted for its onomatopoeia. In West Indies Ltd, he critiqued ongoing racial exploitation. He also wrote the collections Tengo and El son entero.

His poem "Ballad of the Two Grandfathers" ends by describing figures "of equal size" who "shout, dream, weep, sing." The poem opens by describing "Shadows that only I can see" and contrasts the title characters' cries of "I tire!" and "I am dying!" In his poem "Sometimes" (or "A Veces"), he described wanting to bring a flower to his lover.

Guillén was parodied by Guillermo Cabrera Infante in the novel Three Trapped Tigers. Nancy Morejón is an Afro-Cuban poet and scholar of Guillén. Langston Hughes translated two collections of Guillén's Selected Poems, which appeared after his work became known in the English-speaking world.

works.name: Sensemayá (Chant for Killing a Snake)
works.description: Guillén's most frequently clued work, a poem about a snake-killing ritual featuring the onomatopoeic refrain "Mayombe-bombe-mayombe." The poem inspired Silvestre Revueltas's orchestral composition of the same name.

clues.clue: Features the refrain "Mayombe-bombe-mayombe" in a chant about killing a snake
examples: This poet repeatedly exclaimed 'Mayombe-bombe-mayombe' in a 'Chant for Killing a Snake'.
A poet from this ethnic minority imitated its music with onomatopoeia exemplified by the line 'Mayombe-bombe-mayombe' from a 'chant to kill a snake.'

clues.clue: Describes a figure with "eyes of glass" who "coils on a stick" and "can move without feet"; instructs "Hit him with an ax and he dies"
examples: This poet described a figure with 'eyes of glass', who 'coils on a stick' and 'can move without feet' before instructing, 'Hit him with an ax and he dies'.

clues.clue: Inspired Silvestre Revueltas's orchestral composition of the same name, which begins with a bass clarinet trill in 4/8 time
examples: A poem from one of these two countries about killing a snake inspired a composer from the other to write a piece beginning with a bass clarinet trill in 4/8 time.
His poem about a snake-killing ritual inspired the Silvestre Revueltas composition Sensemaya.

clues.clue: Eartha Kitt recorded Langston Hughes's translation of the poem
examples: Eartha Kitt recorded a reading of Hughes's translation of this other Black poet's 'Chant for Killing a Snake.'

works.name: Motivos de son
works.description: Guillén's breakthrough collection of eight short poems based on Afro-Cuban son music. Langston Hughes translated poems from it into Black vernacular English.

clues.clue: Collection of eight short poems based on Afro-Cuban son music that brought Guillén to fame
examples: He first gained fame with eight short poems based on Afro-Cuban 'Son' music.
This author drew on the rhythms of the musical genre son in his collections Motivos de son and El son entero.

clues.clue: First poem addresses an Afro-Cuban boy: "Charity's payin' yo' keep / She's givin' you all you need"
examples: A boy of this ethnicity is told, 'Charity's payin' yo' keep, / She's givin' you all you need' in the first poem of a collection titled for motifs in this ethnic group's music.

clues.clue: Hughes translated "Li'l Manuel" into Black vernacular; Manuel critiqued for "all dat English you used to know"
examples: 'Li'l Manuel' is critiqued for 'all dat English you used to know' in Hughes's Black vernacular translation.

works.name: Ballad of the Two Grandfathers
works.description: A poem contrasting two ancestral figures representing African and Spanish heritage, ending with them described as "of equal size" who "shout, dream, weep, sing."

clues.clue: Ends with figures "of equal size" who "shout, dream, weep, sing"
examples: This poet ended one poem by describing figures 'of equal size' who 'shout, dream, weep, sing'.

clues.clue: Opens with "Shadows that only I can see"; contrasts the title characters' cries of "I tire!" and "I am dying!"
examples: That poem opens by describing 'Shadows that only I can see', and contrasts the title characters' cries of 'I tire!' and 'I am dying!'.

works.name: Other Works and Collections
works.description: Other collections include Songoro cosongo (noted for onomatopoeia), El son entero, West Indies Ltd (critiques racial exploitation), and Tengo. His poem "Sometimes" describes wanting to bring a flower to his lover.

clues.clue: Songoro cosongo is noted for its onomatopoeia
examples: This author of Tengo and the onomatopoetic Songoro cosongo critiqued ongoing racial exploitation in West Indies Ltd.

clues.clue: West Indies Ltd critiques ongoing racial exploitation
examples: This author critiqued ongoing racial exploitation in West Indies Ltd.

clues.clue: "Sometimes" / "A Veces": described wanting to bring a flower to his lover
examples: This poet described wanting to bring a flower to his lover at the end of his poem 'Sometimes' or 'A Veces.'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Cuban poet and journalist of African descent who was declared Cuba's national poet by the Castro government in 1961. His work draws on Afro-Cuban musical forms, particularly son. He had a notable connection with Langston Hughes, who translated his Selected Poems.

clues.clue: Declared Cuba's national poet by the Castro government in 1961; held the position for over two decades
examples: He was the national poet of Cuba for over two decades under the Castro government.
This author was declared Cuba's national poet by the Castro regime in 1961.

clues.clue: Cuban poet and journalist of African descent
examples: Name this poet and journalist of African descent.
For 10 points, name this Cuban poet of 'Ballad of the Two Grandfathers' and 'Sensemaya'.

clues.clue: Langston Hughes translated his Selected Poems; Hughes's translation appeared after Guillén's 1957 death (note: this may be incorrect per sources)
examples: Two collections of Selected Poems, respectively translated by Doris Dana and Langston Hughes, appeared after this poet's 1957 death.

clues.clue: Parodied by Guillermo Cabrera Infante in Three Trapped Tigers
examples: Guillermo Cabrera Infante parodied the writing styles of other Cuban authors... One of those parodies makes fun of this author.

clues.clue: Nancy Morejón is an Afro-Cuban poet and scholar of Guillén
examples: This Afro-Cuban poet and scholar of Nicolas Guillen began successive stanzas 'I rebelled,' 'I walked,' and 'I rose up' in her poem 'Mujer negra.'

cards.front: Poem: features the onomatopoeic refrain "Mayombe-bombe-mayombe" in a chant about killing a snake
Poem: a figure with "eyes of glass" "coils on a stick" and "can move without feet"; the speaker instructs "Hit him with an ax and he dies"
Poem: this poem about a snake-killing ritual inspired a Silvestre Revueltas orchestral composition beginning with a bass clarinet trill in 4/8 time
Poem: Eartha Kitt recorded Langston Hughes's translation of this "Chant for Killing a Snake"
Poetry Collection: eight short poems based on Afro-Cuban son music; Langston Hughes translated "Li'l Manuel" from it into Black vernacular
Poetry Collection: its first poem addresses a boy, telling him "Charity's payin' yo' keep / She's givin' you all you need"
Poem: ends with figures "of equal size" who "shout, dream, weep, sing"; opens with "Shadows that only I can see"
Poem: contrasts two ancestral figures' cries of "I tire!" and "I am dying!"
Poet: declared Cuba's national poet by the Castro government in 1961; held the position for over two decades
Poet: parodied by Guillermo Cabrera Infante in the novel Three Trapped Tigers
Poet: Nancy Morejón is an Afro-Cuban poet and scholar of this author
Poetry Collection: noted for its onomatopoeia; by the same Afro-Cuban poet who wrote Motivos de son
Poetry Collection: critiques ongoing racial exploitation; by the Cuban national poet

summary: Nicolás Guillén (full name Nicolás Cristóbal Guillén Batista) was a Cuban poet and journalist of African descent, declared Cuba's national poet by the Castro government in 1961, a position he held for over two decades.
